differences: Yjs is usually the lower-friction choice when you want a proven CRDT ecosystem and adapter coverage for web collaboration today.
Automerge can be easier to reason about if local-first semantics and offline-first state ownership are central to the product.
The deciding factor is usually not raw CRDT theory; it is which integration path better fits your existing app and rollout risk.
quiz: Which option better matches your current integration path in the existing app?
If collaboration fails mid-session, which tool leaves you with the safer operational fallback?
What would make the extra flexibility of the alternative worth the migration cost?

differences: SQLite is faster to start with when the system is single-user or low-concurrency and deployment simplicity matters most.
Postgres is usually the safer long-term choice once concurrency, multi-user writes, or operational reporting become central.
The real question is not which database is more powerful; it is whether the migration cost later is likely higher than the setup cost now.
quiz: Is the expected write pattern still simple enough that SQLite is a reversible choice?
What future workload would force you off SQLite first?
What is the smallest validation step that would prove Postgres is necessary now?
